app.title: Logic Gates — Interactive Digital Logic
app.subtitle: From transistors to computation: how gates process information
fundamental.intro: Logic gates are the building blocks of all digital circuits. Each gate performs a simple Boolean operation on one or two binary inputs. What makes them remarkable is that every gate is physically built from just a few CMOS transistors on silicon.
gate.not.name: NOT (Inverter)
gate.not.description: Outputs the opposite of its input. The simplest possible gate — just 2 transistors (1 PMOS + 1 NMOS). When input is 1, NMOS pulls output to ground. When input is 0, PMOS pulls output to Vdd.
gate.and.name: AND
gate.and.description: Outputs 1 only when BOTH inputs are 1. Built as NAND + NOT (6 transistors). Not a 'natural' CMOS gate — it needs an extra inverter stage because CMOS naturally inverts.
gate.or.name: OR
gate.or.description: Outputs 1 when EITHER input is 1. Built as NOR + NOT (6 transistors). Like AND, it's not natural in CMOS and requires an extra inverter.
gate.xor.name: XOR (Exclusive OR)
gate.xor.description: Outputs 1 when inputs DIFFER. The most complex basic gate (8-12 transistors). Essential for arithmetic — XOR computes the sum bit in binary addition.
truthTable.title: Truth Table
nand.intro: NAND is functionally complete — every logic gate can be built using NAND gates alone. Since a NAND gate costs only 4 transistors in CMOS, this means you can build ANY digital circuit from a single, cheap building block. This profound result, discovered independently by Peirce (1880) and Sheffer (1913), is the foundation of modern chip design.
nand.nandGate.title: The NAND Gate — Universal Building Block
nand.nandGate.description: NAND outputs 0 only when BOTH inputs are 1. It is the inverse of AND. In CMOS, NAND is a 'natural' gate: 2 PMOS transistors in parallel form the pull-up network, and 2 NMOS transistors in series form the pull-down. This makes it one of the cheapest gates to manufacture.
nand.not.title: NAND → NOT
nand.not.description: The simplest derivation: connect the same wire to both NAND inputs. NAND(0,0) = 1 and NAND(1,1) = 0 — which is exactly NOT. Uses 1 NAND gate (4 transistors), compared to a native NOT gate's 2 transistors.
nand.not.ariaLabel: NOT from NAND: single NAND gate with both inputs tied together
nand.and.title: NAND → AND
nand.and.description: NAND is the opposite of AND, so inverting NAND gives us AND. Gate 1 computes NAND(a,b), then Gate 2 acts as NOT by feeding that result to both inputs of a second NAND. Two NAND gates, 8 transistors total — compared to 6 transistors for a native AND (NAND + inverter).
nand.and.ariaLabel: AND from NAND: two NAND gates in series, second one inverts the first
nand.or.title: NAND → OR
nand.or.description: This is De Morgan's Law in action: A OR B = NOT(NOT(A) AND NOT(B)) = NAND(NOT(A), NOT(B)). Gates 1 and 2 invert each input (using the NOT-from-NAND trick), then Gate 3 NANDs the results. Three NAND gates, 12 transistors — compared to 6 for a native OR.
nand.or.ariaLabel: OR from NAND: two NAND gates invert inputs, third NAND combines them via De Morgan's Law
nand.xor.title: NAND → XOR
nand.xor.description: The most complex basic derivation, requiring 4 NAND gates (16 transistors). Gate 1 computes N = NAND(a,b). This intermediate value feeds into Gates 2 and 3 alongside the original inputs. Gate 4 combines the results. The signal reuse (N goes to two gates) is what makes XOR inherently more expensive than AND or OR.
nand.xor.ariaLabel: XOR from NAND: four NAND gates with shared intermediate wire N
nand.tradeoff.title: The Engineering Tradeoff
nand.tradeoff.description: Building everything from NAND gates uses more transistors than mixing gate types. So why would anyone do it? Uniformity. When every gate on a chip is identical, manufacturing becomes simpler: one mask pattern, one timing model, one test procedure. Early chips used this approach extensively. Modern designs use a mix of gate types for efficiency, but NAND remains the single most common gate in production chips.
